title: CLAUDE.md — UtopiaPlanitia
text: UtopiaPlanitia R package providing variable importance, heterogeneity tests, visualization tools causal forests (built grf). v0.2.0. Think shipyard experimental causal forest tooling. GitHub: CetiAlphaFive/UtopiaPlanitia pkgdown site: cetialphafive.github.io/UtopiaPlanitia

what: Variable Importance, Heterogeneity Tests, and Visualization for Causal Forests
title: Variable Importance, Heterogeneity Tests, and Visualization for Causal Forests
text: package contains various functions construction. Use risk. include variety tests statistics heterogeneous treatment effects variable importance, heterogeneity tests, visualization tools causal forests built grf package.

title: Variable Importance, Heterogeneity Tests, and Visualization for Causal Forests
text: LOCO variable importance causal forests (cf_loco()) ranger models (loco()) Omnibus heterogeneity (HTE) testing combining calibration, high/low CATE, RATE tests (omni_hetero()) Diagnostic, PDP, rank, interaction plots causal forests S3 methods: summary() plot() causal_forest cf_loco objects

title: Variable Importance, Heterogeneity Tests, and Visualization for Causal Forests
text: can install UtopiaPlanitia GitHub :
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("CetiAlphaFive/UtopiaPlanitia")

title: Variable Importance, Heterogeneity Tests, and Visualization for Causal Forests
code: library(grf) library(UtopiaPlanitia)  set.seed(1995) n <- 500; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) colnames(X) <- paste0("X", 1:p) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n)  cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W)  # S3 summary: ATE, variable importance, heterogeneity tests summary(cf)  # S3 plot: diagnostics (default), or type = "rank", "pdp", "inter" plot(cf, type = "rank")  # LOCO variable importance vi <- cf_loco(cf) summary(vi) plot(vi)

what: Modified LOCO Variable Importance for Causal Forests — cf_loco
title: Modified LOCO Variable Importance for Causal Forests — cf_loco
text: Computes leave-one-covariate-(LOCO) variable importance causal forests using debiased method Benard Josse (2023).

title: Modified LOCO Variable Importance for Causal Forests — cf_loco
code: cf_loco(   c.forest,   variable.groups = NULL,   group.by.corr = FALSE,   corr.threshold = 0.5,   normalize = FALSE,   screen = FALSE,   stabilize = 1e-06,   seed = 1995 )

title: Modified LOCO Variable Importance for Causal Forests — cf_loco
text: c.forest fitted causal forest object grf package. variable.groups list variable groups. element list contain variable names group. group..corr Logical indicating whether group variables correlation. Default FALSE. corr.threshold numeric value 0 1 indicating correlation threshold grouping variables. Default 0.5. normalize Logical. TRUE, return VI scores normalized sum 1. Default FALSE. screen Controls optional pre-screening variables via split-frequency importance (grf::variable_importance()), essentially free compute. Screening reduces number expensive LOCO refits. FALSE (default) screening. estimated runtime high session interactive, user prompted screen. TRUE Auto-screen. Keep variables split-frequency importance mean. Integer k Keep top-k variables split-frequency importance. Screened-variables receive importance = 0 output. stabilize Numeric. Floor per-observation denominator Var_alpha(W.centered) CATE re-estimation step. Prevents division--zero forest weights concentrate units near-identical propensity scores. Default 1e-6. Set 0 disable. seed integer seed reproducibility. Default 1995.

title: Modified LOCO Variable Importance for Causal Forests — cf_loco
text: object class "cf_loco" components: vimp Data frame columns Variable Importance. normalized Logical indicating whether scores normalized. n Number observations. p Number covariates.

title: Modified LOCO Variable Importance for Causal Forests — cf_loco
text: LOCO measures. Leave-one-covariate-(LOCO) importance drops covariate (group covariates) model, refits forest without , measures much worse individualized predictions become. large score means variable important predicting heterogeneous treatment effects; score near zero means forest can recover CATEs without variable. Debiasing. Naive LOCO biased refitting forest introduces retrain variance. implementation uses debiased estimator Benard Josse (2023): full-data refit used bias correction term, per-observation CATE re-estimation performed via forest weights rather raw OOB predictions. Computational cost. function refits p + 1 causal forests (one per dropped variable plus one full-data refit), hyperparameters original. large datasets many covariates, consider using screen argument reduce number refits. Normalization. normalize = TRUE, negative importance scores (can occur dropping variable improves predictions) set zero, remaining scores rescaled sum one. gives proportional importance interpretation discards directional information. Variable grouping. Highly correlated covariates can grouped dropped together. Use group..corr = TRUE automatic grouping via correlation threshold, supply manual list via variable.groups.

title: Modified LOCO Variable Importance for Causal Forests — cf_loco
text: Benard, C. Josse, J. (2023). Variable Importance Causal Forests: Breaking Heterogeneity Treatment Effects. doi:10.48550/arXiv.2308.03369 Athey, S., Tibshirani, J., Wager, S. (2019). Generalized Random Forests. Annals Statistics, 47(2), 1148–1178. doi:10.1214/18-AOS1709

title: Modified LOCO Variable Importance for Causal Forests — cf_loco
code: # \donttest{ library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 200; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) colnames(X) <- paste0("X", seq_len(p)) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 100) vi <- cf_loco(cf) summary(vi) #> LOCO Variable Importance (Benard and Josse, 2023) #>   n = 200  p = 5  #>   Normalized: FALSE  #>  #>  Variable Importance #>        X1   0.976803 #>        X2  -0.008422 #>        X3  -0.008641 #>        X5  -0.012525 #>        X4  -0.013253 plot(vi)   # Normalized importance (sums to 1) vi_norm <- cf_loco(cf, normalize = TRUE) summary(vi_norm) #> LOCO Variable Importance (Benard and Josse, 2023) #>   n = 200  p = 5  #>   Normalized: TRUE  #>  #>  Variable Importance #>        X1          1 #>        X2          0 #>        X3          0 #>        X4          0 #>        X5          0 # }

title: Compute Variable Importance (Internal) — compute_vimp
text: Computes single variable importance score forest weights centered data. internal helper called cf_loco() dropped-variable refit; users call cf_loco() directly.

title: Compute Variable Importance (Internal) — compute_vimp
code: compute_vimp(alpha, Y.centered, W.centered, tau.hat, stabilize = 1e-06)

title: Compute Variable Importance (Internal) — compute_vimp
text: alpha Sparse weight matrix grf::get_forest_weights(). row gives weights forest observations receive predicting unit 's treatment effect. Y.centered Numeric vector. Outcome residuals Y - Y.hat, Y.hat forest's outcome model (marginal mean Y given X). W.centered Numeric vector. Treatment residuals W - W.hat, W.hat estimated propensity score. tau.hat Numeric vector. Original --bag CATE predictions full (non-dropped) causal forest. stabilize Numeric floor per-observation denominator Var_alpha(W.centered). Prevents division--zero forest weights concentrate units near-identical propensity scores. Default 1e-6. Set 0 disable.

title: Compute Variable Importance (Internal) — compute_vimp
text: single numeric value: importance score refit. Higher values indicate greater importance.

title: Compute Variable Importance (Internal) — compute_vimp
text: observation, function re-estimates CATE using weight-based regression: $$\hat{\tau}_i^{new} = \frac{Cov_\alpha(W_i, Y_i) - \hat{\tau}_i \cdot Var_\alpha(W_i)}{Var_\alpha(W_i)}$$ \(Cov_\alpha\) \(Var_\alpha\) weighted covariances variances using forest weights \(\alpha\). importance score normalized squared difference original re-estimated CATEs, divided variance original predictions. Var_alpha(W.centered) near zero observations (e.g., forest weights concentrate units similar propensity scores), stabilize parameter clamps denominator prevent numerical instability.

title: Compute Variable Importance (Internal) — compute_vimp
text: Benard, C. Josse, J. (2023). Variable Importance Causal Forests: Breaking Heterogeneity Treatment Effects. doi:10.48550/arXiv.2308.03369

what: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
title: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
text: Computes leave-one-covariate-(LOCO) variable importance ranger random forests. Two modes available: split-sample LOCO conformal inference (confidence intervals p-values), OOB-based LOCO (point estimates ).

title: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
code: loco(model, alpha = 0.1, split = TRUE, seed = 1995, verbose = FALSE)

title: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
text: model fitted ranger object (must fit keep.inbag = TRUE using OOB mode, training data must recoverable call). alpha Significance level conformal inference intervals. Default 0.1 (90% intervals). used split = TRUE. split Logical. TRUE (default), uses split-sample LOCO via conformalInference::loco(). FALSE, uses OOB prediction error differences. seed Integer seed reproducibility. Default 1995. verbose Logical. Print progress conformal inference? Default FALSE. used split = TRUE.

title: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
text: data frame sorted descending importance columns: variable Covariate name. importance LOCO importance score (midpoint CI split = TRUE, increase OOB error split = FALSE). ci.lower Lower confidence bound (split = TRUE). ci.upper Upper confidence bound (split = TRUE). p.value P-value conformal Z-test (split = TRUE).

title: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
text: Choosing mode. split-sample method (split = TRUE) provides valid confidence intervals p-values via conformal inference, slower splits data internally. OOB method (split = FALSE) faster suitable screening exploratory analysis, provides point estimates formal inference. Split-sample mode requires conformalInference package, available GitHub:   OOB mode refits original ranger model per covariate, time dropping one predictor, compares OOB prediction error full-model baseline. importance score increase OOB error variable removed; larger values indicate important variables.
code: devtools::install_github("ryantibs/conformal", subdir = "conformalInference")

title: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
text: Lei, J., G'Sell, M., Rinaldo, ., Tibshirani, R. J., & Wasserman, L. (2018). Distribution-Free Predictive Inference Regression. Journal American Statistical Association, 113(523), 1094–1111. Rinaldo, ., Wasserman, L., & G'Sell, M. (2019). Bootstrapping Sample Splitting High-Dimensional, Assumption-Lean Inference. Annals Statistics, 47(6), 3438–3469.

title: LOCO Variable Importance for Outcome Models — loco
code: # \donttest{ if (requireNamespace("ranger", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(1995)   dat <- data.frame(y = rnorm(100), x1 = rnorm(100), x2 = rnorm(100))   mod <- ranger::ranger(y ~ ., data = dat, num.trees = 50)   loco(mod, split = FALSE) } #>   variable importance #> 1       x2  0.3322422 #> 2       x1  0.2607941 # }

title: Omnibus Tests of Heterogeneity — omni_hetero
text: Performs various heterogeneity tests fitted causal forest model. Combines calibration test Chernozhukov et al. (2018), naive high/low CATE split (Athey Wager, 2019), sequential RATE test (Wager, 2024), OOB RATE heuristics single summary table.

title: Omnibus Tests of Heterogeneity — omni_hetero
code: omni_hetero(c.forest, seed = 1995)

title: Omnibus Tests of Heterogeneity — omni_hetero
text: c.forest fitted causal forest object grf package. seed integer seed reproducibility. Default 1995. Controls fold assignment sequential RATE test.

title: Omnibus Tests of Heterogeneity — omni_hetero
text: data frame one row per test following columns: heterogeneity_test Character. Name citation test. estimate Numeric. test statistic effect estimate. NA sequential RATE test (produces p-value). p_value Numeric. Two-sided p-value (one-sided final row). Small values indicate evidence treatment effect heterogeneity. hetero_detected Logical. TRUE p_value <= 0.05.

title: Omnibus Tests of Heterogeneity — omni_hetero
text: function combines five tests treatment effect heterogeneity, ranging well-calibrated heuristic: Calibration test (Chernozhukov et al., 2018): Regresses doubly robust scores forest's CATE predictions. "differential forest prediction" coefficient tests whether forest captures meaningful heterogeneity beyond ATE. High vs. low CATE (Athey Wager, 2019): Splits units median predicted CATE compares ATE half. significant difference suggests forest detects real variation. Sequential RATE (Wager, 2024): k-fold cross-validated test correct size (valid Type error). trustworthy test battery preferred formal inference. OOB RATE, two-sided (heuristic): Uses --bag CATE predictions directly. Known anti-conservative (~30\ null nominal 5\ screening tool, formal inference. OOB RATE, one-sided (heuristic): One-sided version . Approximately valid direction heterogeneity pre-specified.

title: Omnibus Tests of Heterogeneity — omni_hetero
text: Chernozhukov, V., Demirer, M., Duflo, E., Fernandez-Val, . (2018). Generic Machine Learning Inference Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Randomized Experiments. NBER Working Paper 24678. Athey, S. Wager, S. (2019). Estimating Treatment Effects Causal Forests: Application. Observational Studies, 5, 37–51. Wager, S. (2024). Sequential Validation Treatment Heterogeneity. doi:10.48550/arXiv.2405.05534

title: Omnibus Tests of Heterogeneity — omni_hetero
code: # \donttest{ library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 200; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 100) omni_hetero(cf) #>                                   heterogeneity_test  estimate      p_value #> 1       Calibration Test (Chernozhukov et al., 2018) 1.3212439 2.949514e-09 #> 2          High vs. Low CATE (Athey and Wager, 2019) 1.6997625 2.250167e-08 #> 3                      Sequential RATE (Wager, 2024)        NA          NaN #> 4 OOB RATE, two-sided (heuristic, anti-conservative) 0.5295879 9.115004e-04 #> 5                    OOB RATE, one-sided (heuristic) 0.5295879 4.557502e-04 #>   hetero_detected #> 1            TRUE #> 2            TRUE #> 3              NA #> 4            TRUE #> 5            TRUE # }

title: Plot a Causal Forest — plot.causal_forest
text: Dispatches one package's plot functions based type.

title: Plot a Causal Forest — plot.causal_forest
code: # S3 method for class 'causal_forest' plot(x, type = c("diag", "pdp", "scatter", "rank", "inter"), ...)

title: Plot a Causal Forest — plot.causal_forest
text: x fitted causal forest object grf package. type Character string specifying plot type. One : "diag": multi-panel diagnostics (plot_diag()) "pdp": partial dependence plot (plot_pdp()) "scatter": individual CATE scatter (plot_scatter()) "rank": ranked CATEs CIs (plot_rank()) "inter": (deprecated) hex interaction plot (plot_inter()) ... Additional arguments passed underlying plot function (e.g., x_var "pdp", "scatter", "inter").

title: Plot a Causal Forest — plot.causal_forest
text: plot object (return value dispatched function).

title: Plot a Causal Forest — plot.causal_forest
text: convenience dispatcher. type targets different diagnostic question: "diag" nuisance models (outcome, propensity) well calibrated? treatment effect heterogeneity? "pdp" CATE change function one two covariates, marginalizing rest? "scatter" individual OOB CATE predictions look like single covariate? "rank" distribution individual CATEs, sorted magnitude, confidence intervals? "inter" (Deprecated) Hex-binned interaction plot; use "pdp" y_var argument instead.

title: Plot a Causal Forest — plot.causal_forest
code: library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 200; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) colnames(X) <- paste0("X", seq_len(p)) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 100) plot(cf, type = "rank")

title: Plot LOCO Variable Importance — plot.cf_loco
text: Draws horizontal lollipop chart LOCO variable importance scores, sorted least important (bottom top).

title: Plot LOCO Variable Importance — plot.cf_loco
code: # S3 method for class 'cf_loco' plot(x, ...)

title: Plot LOCO Variable Importance — plot.cf_loco
text: x object class "cf_loco" returned cf_loco(). ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: Plot LOCO Variable Importance — plot.cf_loco
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot LOCO Variable Importance — plot.cf_loco
text: Variables displayed horizontal segments ending points, length proportional importance. normalize = TRUE used cf_loco(), y-axis label changes "Importance (normalized)".

title: Plot LOCO Variable Importance — plot.cf_loco
code: library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 200; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) colnames(X) <- paste0("X", seq_len(p)) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 100) vi <- cf_loco(cf) plot(vi)

what: Model Diagnostics for Causal Forest — plot_diag
title: Model Diagnostics for Causal Forest — plot_diag
text: Generates multi-panel diagnostics figure causal forest model, covering outcome model fit, treatment balance, CATE distribution.

title: Model Diagnostics for Causal Forest — plot_diag
code: plot_diag(c.forest)

title: Model Diagnostics for Causal Forest — plot_diag
text: c.forest fitted causal forest object grf package.

title: Model Diagnostics for Causal Forest — plot_diag
text: list containing following elements: out_check ggplot2 object showing density plot comparing real Y predicted Y. bal_perm_plot ggplot2 object showing treatment propensity plot. cate_distro_plot ggplot2 object showing density plot distribution CATEs. rmse_out Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) outcome model predictions. Also printed console.

title: Model Diagnostics for Causal Forest — plot_diag
text: figure contains three panels: Outcome check (top-left): Overlays density observed outcome Y forest's outcome model predictions Y.hat. Good overlap indicates well-specified outcome model. RMSE printed console. Balance permutation test (top-right): Uses MLbalance::random_check() test whether treatment assignment W predictable covariates X. non-significant result (large p-value) consistent random --random assignment. See Gagnon-Bartsch Shem-Tov (2019). CATE distribution (bottom): Histogram interval summary --bag CATE estimates. tight distribution centered zero suggests little heterogeneity; spread distribution suggests meaningful variation treatment effects across units.

title: Model Diagnostics for Causal Forest — plot_diag
text: Gagnon-Bartsch, J. Shem-Tov, Y. (2019). Classification Permutation Test: Flexible Approach Testing Covariate Imbalance Observational Experimental Studies. doi:10.48550/arXiv.1903.07841

title: Model Diagnostics for Causal Forest — plot_diag
code: library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 200; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 100) plot_diag(cf) #> RMSE for outcome model: 1.136845  #> No Simulated Assignment Vector Provided, Null Distribution Generated Using Permutated Treatment Assignment.  #> $outcome_check_plot  #>  #> $treatment_propensity_plot  #>  #> $cates_distribution_plot  #>  #> $rmse_out #> [1] 1.136845 #>

title: Interaction Plot for Causal Forest (Deprecated) — plot_inter
code: plot_inter(   c.forest,   x_var,   y_var,   bin_count = 50,   limits = c(min(c.forest$predictions), max(c.forest$predictions)) )

title: Interaction Plot for Causal Forest (Deprecated) — plot_inter
text: c.forest fitted causal forest model object grf package. x_var name variable plotted x-axis. y_var name variable plotted y-axis. bin_count Number bins hexagonal binning. Default 50. limits limits color scale, default minimum maximum CATEs.

title: Interaction Plot for Causal Forest (Deprecated) — plot_inter
text: ggplot2 object interaction plot.

title: Interaction Plot for Causal Forest (Deprecated) — plot_inter
text: plot_inter() plots raw OOB CATEs hex-binned means. proper two-way partial dependence plot, use plot_pdp() x_var y_var.

title: Interaction Plot for Causal Forest (Deprecated) — plot_inter
code: library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 200; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) colnames(X) <- paste0("X", seq_len(p)) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 100) plot_inter(cf, x_var = "X1", y_var = "X2") #> Warning: plot_inter() is deprecated. Use plot_pdp(c.forest, x_var, y_var) for a 2-way partial dependence plot.

what: Partial Dependence Plot for Causal Forest — plot_pdp
title: Partial Dependence Plot for Causal Forest — plot_pdp
text: Computes plots partial dependence CATE one two covariates fitted causal forest. Unlike plot_scatter(), shows individual OOB CATEs, marginalizes remaining covariates averaging predictions across held-sample grid point (Friedman, 2001).

title: Partial Dependence Plot for Causal Forest — plot_pdp
code: plot_pdp(   c.forest,   x_var,   y_var = NULL,   grid_size = 50,   n_max = 2000,   show_ate_region = TRUE,   show_scatter = TRUE,   trim = TRUE,   x.limits = NULL,   y.limits = NULL,   color.var = NULL,   color.cat = NULL,   color.lab = NULL,   xlab = NULL,   num.threads = NULL )

title: Partial Dependence Plot for Causal Forest — plot_pdp
text: c.forest fitted causal forest model object grf package. x_var Character. Name primary covariate. y_var Character NULL. supplied, 2-way PDP (tile plot) produced; otherwise 1-way PDP (line plot). grid_size Integer. Number grid points per variable (default 50). 2-way PDP grid grid_size^2 points, moderate values recommended. n_max Integer. Maximum rows covariate matrix used averaging. nrow(X.orig) > n_max, random subsample drawn. Default 2000 1-way PDP 500 2-way PDP (PD averaging converges fast; pass n_max explicitly override). show_ate_region Logical. Draw ATE +/- 1.96 SE band? used 1-way PDP (default TRUE). show_scatter Logical. Overlay individual OOB CATEs 1-way PDP? (default TRUE). trim Logical. 2-way PDP : mask grid points outside convex hull training data avoid extrapolation unsupported regions (default TRUE). Uses grDevices::chull(). Ignored 1-way PDP. x.limits x axis limits specified c() vector. Defaults range grid. y.limits y axis limits specified c() vector. Defaults range grid. color.var Character NULL. Name covariate split 1-way PDP . separate PD curve drawn level. 1-way PDP. color.cat Character vector NULL. Labels levels color.var, order sorted unique values. NULL, raw values used labels. color.lab Character NULL. Legend title grouping variable. Defaults color.var. xlab Character NULL. Custom x-axis label. Defaults x_var. num.threads Integer NULL. Number threads predict(). Passed directly grf::predict.causal_forest(). NULL (default) uses available threads.

title: Partial Dependence Plot for Causal Forest — plot_pdp
text: utopia_plot object (ggExtraPlot marginal histograms).

title: Partial Dependence Plot for Causal Forest — plot_pdp
text: Computational cost. grid point requires full predict() call (possibly subsampled) covariate matrix. 2-way PDPs, grid points outside convex hull training data filtered prediction trim = TRUE, typically removes 30-50\ Combined lower default n_max (500 vs 2000), yields ~6-7x speedup evaluating full grid_size^2 grid.

title: Partial Dependence Plot for Causal Forest — plot_pdp
text: Friedman, J. H. (2001). Greedy Function Approximation: Gradient Boosting Machine. Annals Statistics, 29(5), 1189–1232.

title: Partial Dependence Plot for Causal Forest — plot_pdp
code: library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 200; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) colnames(X) <- paste0("X", seq_len(p)) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 100) plot_pdp(cf, x_var = "X1")  plot_pdp(cf, x_var = "X1", y_var = "X2", grid_size = 20) #> 191 of 400 grid points trimmed (outside convex hull)

what: Rank Plot of Individual CATE Estimates — plot_rank
title: Rank Plot of Individual CATE Estimates — plot_rank
text: Generates rank plot individual CATE (Conditional Average Treatment Effect) estimates fitted causal forest, sorted magnitude 95\

title: Rank Plot of Individual CATE Estimates — plot_rank
code: plot_rank(c.forest, show_ate_region = TRUE)  rank_plot(c.forest, show_ate_region = TRUE)

title: Rank Plot of Individual CATE Estimates — plot_rank
text: c.forest fitted causal forest object grf package. show_ate_region Logical. TRUE (default), draws horizontal band showing ATE +/- 1.96 standard errors. Units whose CIs fall entirely outside band strong evidence individual-level heterogeneity.

title: Rank Plot of Individual CATE Estimates — plot_rank
text: ggplot2 object. Individual CATEs plotted y-axis, ranked magnitude x-axis, color indicating effect direction magnitude (red = negative, green = positive).

title: Rank Plot of Individual CATE Estimates — plot_rank
text: point represents one unit's --bag CATE estimate, 95\ confidence interval derived forest's variance estimates (estimate.variance = TRUE grf::predict.causal_forest()). Units sorted negative positive treatment effect. Interpretation. flat rank plot (CIs overlapping ATE band) suggests homogeneous effects. steep S-curve CIs separating ATE band suggests meaningful heterogeneity — units benefit much (less) average. color gradient reinforces : red points units harmed unaffected treatment, green points units benefit .

title: Rank Plot of Individual CATE Estimates — plot_rank
text: Athey, S. Wager, S. (2019). Estimating Treatment Effects Causal Forests: Application. Observational Studies, 5, 37–51. Wager, S. Athey, S. (2018). Estimation Inference Heterogeneous Treatment Effects Using Random Forests. Journal American Statistical Association, 113(523), 1228–1242. doi:10.1080/01621459.2017.1319839

title: Rank Plot of Individual CATE Estimates — plot_rank
code: library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 200; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 100) plot_rank(cf)

what: CATE Scatter Plot for Causal Forest — plot_scatter
title: CATE Scatter Plot for Causal Forest — plot_scatter
text: Plots individual --bag CATE estimates covariate optional loess smoother ATE confidence region. Friedman (2001) partial dependence plot — see plot_pdp() .

title: CATE Scatter Plot for Causal Forest — plot_scatter
code: plot_scatter(   c.forest,   x_var,   curve_fitter = TRUE,   method = "loess",   show_ate_region = TRUE,   x.limits = NULL,   y.limits = NULL )

title: CATE Scatter Plot for Causal Forest — plot_scatter
text: c.forest fitted causal forest model object grf package. x_var name variable plotted x-axis. curve_fitter Logical indicating whether include curve fitter. Default TRUE. method smoothing method used geom_smooth curve_fitter TRUE. Default "loess". show_ate_region Logical indicating whether show ATE region (confidence interval). Default TRUE. x.limits x axis limits specified c() vector. Defaults range X. y.limits y axis limits specified c() vector. Defaults range Y.

title: CATE Scatter Plot for Causal Forest — plot_scatter
text: utopia_plot object (ggExtraPlot marginal histograms).

title: CATE Scatter Plot for Causal Forest — plot_scatter
text: point one unit's --bag CATE prediction plotted observed covariate value. Unlike plot_pdp(), marginalizes remaining covariates averaging predictions grid point, plot shows raw individual predictions — making faster sensitive confounding covariates. optional LOESS smoother (method via method) helps reveal conditional relationship covariate treatment effect. smoother deviates ATE band, suggests covariate modifies treatment effect.

title: CATE Scatter Plot for Causal Forest — plot_scatter
text: Friedman, J. H. (2001). Greedy Function Approximation: Gradient Boosting Machine. Annals Statistics, 29(5), 1189–1232.

title: CATE Scatter Plot for Causal Forest — plot_scatter
code: library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 200; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) colnames(X) <- paste0("X", seq_len(p)) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 100) plot_scatter(cf, x_var = "X1") #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x' #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Summarize a Causal Forest — summary.causal_forest
text: Produces one-stop summary fitted causal forest: average treatment effect (ATE), split-frequency variable importance, battery heterogeneity tests.

title: Summarize a Causal Forest — summary.causal_forest
code: # S3 method for class 'causal_forest' summary(object, seed = 1995, ...)

title: Summarize a Causal Forest — summary.causal_forest
text: object fitted causal forest object grf package. seed Integer seed reproducibility. Default 1995. Passed omni_hetero() sequential RATE fold assignment. ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: Summarize a Causal Forest — summary.causal_forest
text: object class "summary.causal_forest" (returned invisibly) components: ate Named numeric vector estimate std.err grf::average_treatment_effect(). vimp Named numeric vector split-frequency variable importance scores grf::variable_importance(). reflect often variable used tree splits — fast heuristic, debiased LOCO scores cf_loco(). heterogeneity Data frame omni_hetero() five heterogeneity tests.

title: Summarize a Causal Forest — summary.causal_forest
text: method assembles three diagnostics: ATE: doubly robust average treatment effect estimate grf. Variable importance: grf's native split-frequency scores, measure often covariate selected splitting. fast compute formal statistical interpretation. debiased LOCO importance proper methodology, use cf_loco(). Heterogeneity tests: full omni_hetero() battery, including calibration test, high/low CATE comparison, sequential RATE, OOB RATE heuristics. See omni_hetero() details test.

title: Summarize a Causal Forest — summary.causal_forest
text: Athey, S., Tibshirani, J., Wager, S. (2019). Generalized Random Forests. Annals Statistics, 47(2), 1148–1178. doi:10.1214/18-AOS1709

title: Summarize a Causal Forest — summary.causal_forest
code: # \donttest{ library(grf) set.seed(1995) n <- 200; p <- 5 X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p), n, p) colnames(X) <- paste0("X", seq_len(p)) W <- rbinom(n, 1, 0.5) Y <- X[, 1] * W + rnorm(n) cf <- causal_forest(X, Y, W, num.trees = 100) summary(cf) #> Average Treatment Effect #>   Estimate: -0.0927  SE: 0.1631  #>  #> Variable Importance (grf native) #>  variable importance #>        X1     0.6322 #>        X4     0.0975 #>        X5     0.0861 #>        X3     0.0715 #>        X2     0.0687 #>  #> Heterogeneity Tests #>                                  heterogeneity_test  estimate      p_value #>        Calibration Test (Chernozhukov et al., 2018) 1.3212439 2.949514e-09 #>           High vs. Low CATE (Athey and Wager, 2019) 1.6997625 2.250167e-08 #>                       Sequential RATE (Wager, 2024)        NA          NaN #>  OOB RATE, two-sided (heuristic, anti-conservative) 0.5295879 9.115004e-04 #>                     OOB RATE, one-sided (heuristic) 0.5295879 4.557502e-04 #>  hetero_detected #>             TRUE #>             TRUE #>               NA #>             TRUE #>             TRUE # }

title: UtopiaPlanitia 0.3.0
text: New plot_pdp(): real Friedman (2001) partial dependence plots 1-way 2-way CATE surfaces. New plot_scatter(): renamed old plot_pdp(), plotted individual OOB CATEs vs. covariate. plot_inter() deprecated favor plot_pdp(c.forest, x_var, y_var). plot.causal_forest() gains type = "scatter" new plot_scatter().

title: UtopiaPlanitia 0.2.0
text: Added S3 summary() plot() methods causal_forest objects. Added S3 summary(), print(), plot() methods cf_loco output. Added plot.causal_forest() dispatcher plot_diag(), plot_pdp(), rank_plot(), plot_inter(). Fixed deprecated aes_string() usage across plot functions. Fixed bare namespace references plot_diag(), plot_rank(), plot_inter(). Replaced size linewidth geom_smooth() calls. Pruned 13 8 core exported functions; archived extras R/old/. Added GitHub Actions R-CMD-check, pkgdown, test coverage, linting. Switched license MIT GPL-3.

title: UtopiaPlanitia 0.1.0
text: Initial release cf_loco(), omni_hetero(), plot functions.
